onlyOne.note: "Chinese writing is indeed a unicase writing system—it does not have the distinction between uppercase and lowercase" — Wikipedia: Unicase
"the classifier gè (个/個)...also serves as a general classifier, which may often be used in place of other classifiers" — Wikipedia: Chinese classifier
"When a noun is preceded by a numeral..., the use of a classifier or measure word is in most cases considered mandatory" — Wikipedia: Chinese classifier / Measure word
"Xiānshēng (先生)...can either follow the surname...In modern Standard Chinese, it is also used...as the title 'Mr'"; nǚshì used for women — Wikipedia: Chinese titles
"Chinese characters do not represent their pronunciation, making it impossible to predict how to read a character based solely on its shape" — Wikipedia: Chinese character sounds

"a semantic component gives a broad category of meaning, while a phonetic component suggests the sound"; but relationship "largely broken down" over time — Wikipedia: Semantic-phonetic compound / Chinese character sounds
"Mandarin Chinese has aspect equipped with aspectual particles (e.g., le, zhe, guo)...signal grammatical aspects" — Wikipedia: Chinese particles / Chinese grammar
"Chinese has no tenses, no voices, and no grammatical number"; "Chinese does not have grammatical markers of tense" — Wikipedia: Chinese grammar
"Wrong word order is a characteristic of Chinglish"; Westernised Chinese direct glosses from English "pose comprehension issues" — Wikipedia: Chinglish / Westernised Chinese language
"Mandarin Chinese is organized around syllables that are created by an initial followed by a final" — Wikipedia (syllable structure, Mandarin phonology sources)
"The ISO adopted pinyin as the standard romanization for modern Chinese in 1982 (ISO 7098:1982...)" — Wikipedia: Pinyin
"The diacritic is placed over the letter that represents the syllable nucleus" — Wikipedia: Pinyin
"The singular pronouns in Mandarin are wǒ 我 'I', nǐ 你 'you', nín 您 'you (formal)', and tā..." — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Chinese
"the second-person singular pronoun 你 (nǐ) can be substituted with its polite form 您 (nín)...to express politeness" — Wikipedia: Pronouns in Chinese

"honorific language in Chinese is achieved by using honorific...alternatives, prefixing/suffixing...or dropping casual-sounding words" vs. Japanese/Korean's elaborate grammatical systems — Wikipedia: Chinese honorifics
"ba (吧) is used for soliciting approval, softening an imperative...used to form first-person imperatives, equivalent to 'let's...'" — Wikipedia: Sentence-final particle
"yes-no questions can be marked with the sentence-final particle 'ma'" — Wikipedia: Sentence-final particle / Chinese grammar
"Mandarin Chinese also has a so-called neutral or floating tone (T0)" — Wikipedia: Standard Chinese phonology
"four lexical tones (T1, high level; T2, rising; T3, falling-rising; T4, falling) as well as a neutral tone" — Wikipedia: Standard Chinese phonology
"two phonemic tones are considered contrastive if there exists a minimal pair"; ma = mother/hemp/horse/scold by tone — Wikipedia: Minimal pair / Tone (linguistics)
"Modifiers usually precede the word they modify, so that adjectives precede nouns" — Wikipedia: Chinese adjectives
"Chinese is classified as an SVO (subject–verb–object) language" — Wikipedia: Chinese grammar
"Chinese can be considered a topic-prominent language: there is a strong preference for sentences that begin with the topic..." — Wikipedia: Chinese grammar
"Chinese sentences are traditionally written as strings of characters, with no marks between words" — Wikipedia: Chinese word-segmented writing
"different segmentations of a given Chinese string can represent very different meanings" (乒乓球拍卖完了 example) — Wikipedia: Chinese word-segmented writing
"the layout used in Western scripts...became predominant in mainland China, where it was mandated by the Chinese government in 1955" — Wikipedia: Horizontal and vertical writing in East Asian scripts
"Chinese characters are logographs...representing the smallest units of meaning called morphemes" — Wikipedia: Chinese character classification / Writing system
"Simplified Chinese characters are the standard forms used in mainland China, Malaysia, and Singapore" — Wikipedia: Simplified Chinese characters
